question: Define a stationary time series. List two properties that must be constant over time for a process to be considered weakly stationary.
expectedPoints: A stationary time series is one whose statistical properties do not change over time.
The mean of the process must be constant and not depend on time.
The variance of the process must be constant and finite (homoscedasticity).
The covariance between observations at two time points depends only on the lag between them, not on the actual time points.

question: Describe the Autoregressive (AR) model. Explain how its order 'p' is determined and what the Yule-Walker equations are used for in this context.
expectedPoints: An AR(p) model expresses a value at time t as a linear combination of its previous p values plus a white noise error term: X_t = c + φ₁X_{t-1} + ... + φ_pX_{t-p} + ε_t.
The order 'p' is the number of lagged observations included in the model and is often determined by analyzing the Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF), which should cut off after lag p.
The Yule-Walker equations are a set of linear equations used to estimate the autoregressive parameters (φ₁, ..., φ_p) by relating the autocorrelations of the process to the parameters.

question: Compare and contrast the Autoregressive (AR) and Moving Average (MA) models. Discuss how the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) and Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF) behave for each model and explain why this is crucial for model identification.
expectedPoints: An AR model regresses on its own past values, while an MA model is a linear regression of the current value against the past shock values.
For an AR(p) model, the ACF decays gradually (tails off) while the PACF has a sharp cut-off after lag p. This cut-off helps identify the order 'p'.
For an MA(q) model, the ACF has a sharp cut-off after lag q, which helps identify the order 'q', while the PACF decays gradually (tails off).
This contrasting behavior of the ACF and PACF is the cornerstone of the Box-Jenkins methodology for identifying the appropriate type and order of a linear stationary model from the observed data.
